headline: Tanjung Piai Wetlands Erosion Accelerates to 8 Metres Per Year
context: NAHRIM monitoring shows the coastline at Tanjung Piai National Park — Malaysia's southernmost point — is eroding at 8 metres per year, up from 3 metres in 2015.

cards.big: Tanjung Piai is Malaysia's southernmost point and a RAMSAR wetland.
cards.sub: Erosion at 8 metres per year means the park will lose its visitor boardwalk within 5 years.

cards.big: Shipping traffic in the Johor Strait increased 23% since 2018.
cards.sub: Wake erosion from larger vessels is contributing to mangrove loss along the park's western edge.

cards.big: Mangrove replanting survival rates at Tanjung Piai are only 22%.
cards.sub: Wave energy exceeds seedling tolerance. Replanting without breakwater protection is futile.

cards.big: The park received RM4.5M in rehabilitation funds since 2018.
cards.sub: 87% was spent on replanting with 22% survival. Alternative approaches like reef breakwaters were not attempted.

cards.big: RM4.5M spent. 22% survival rate. Repeating the same approach and expecting different results is not conservation.
Coastal protection at Tanjung Piai needs engineered solutions — reef breakwaters, sediment trapping — before biological restoration can succeed. The sequence matters.
